critic_raw: The experimental design for hierarchical attention-based summarization has five significant methodological concerns. First, the primary evaluation is human scoring on clarity and informativeness, but the design provides insufficient detail on human evaluation protocols: inter-annotator agreement measurement, number of raters per document, handling of disagreements, and whether evaluators are blind to which system produced each summary. Without these controls, human evaluation is unreliable and non-reproducible. Second, using ROUGE-based tuning on the validation set for model selection while using human scores as the primary reporting metric creates an optimization-evaluation mismatch. ROUGE correlates poorly with human judgments of quality, and a model selected to maximize ROUGE may not be the best model by the human evaluation standard. Third, the stratified random split on an arXiv/PubMed dataset does not account for author-level or paper-level clusters—papers from the same author or research group may appear in both training and test sets, inflating performance if the model learns author-specific writing patterns. Fourth, using existing abstracts as training labels is problematic because abstracts in the wild vary widely in their faithfulness to and coverage of the paper content; some abstracts are highly selective or promotional rather than representative. Fifth, the comparison against extractive summarization as the baseline is confounded by the task: human evaluators tend to rate abstractive fluency more highly than extractive coherence, meaning some of the observed advantage may reflect mode preference rather than quality of the hierarchical mechanism.
all_issues_raised: Human evaluation protocol is underspecified: no inter-annotator agreement, blinding, or rater count
ROUGE-based model selection misaligns with human evaluation as the primary metric
No author/paper-level deduplication in the split—same-author papers in train and test inflate scores
Using existing abstracts as training labels ignores variability in abstract quality and faithfulness
Extractive vs. abstractive baseline conflates architectural mode preference with summarization quality

round_transcripts.critic: The experimental design for hierarchical attention-based summarization has five significant methodological concerns. First, the primary evaluation is human scoring on clarity and informativeness, but the design provides insufficient detail on human evaluation protocols: inter-annotator agreement measurement, number of raters per document, handling of disagreements, and whether evaluators are blind to which system produced each summary. Without these controls, human evaluation is unreliable and non-reproducible. Second, using ROUGE-based tuning on the validation set for model selection while using human scores as the primary reporting metric creates an optimization-evaluation mismatch. ROUGE correlates poorly with human judgments of quality, and a model selected to maximize ROUGE may not be the best model by the human evaluation standard. Third, the stratified random split on an arXiv/PubMed dataset does not account for author-level or paper-level clusters—papers from the same author or research group may appear in both training and test sets, inflating performance if the model learns author-specific writing patterns. Fourth, using existing abstracts as training labels is problematic because abstracts in the wild vary widely in their faithfulness to and coverage of the paper content; some abstracts are highly selective or promotional rather than representative. Fifth, the comparison against extractive summarization as the baseline is confounded by the task: human evaluators tend to rate abstractive fluency more highly than extractive coherence, meaning some of the observed advantage may reflect mode preference rather than quality of the hierarchical mechanism.
round_transcripts.defender: The defense addresses two issues. On human evaluation protocol: the design states 'human evaluators with diverse backgrounds and disciplined grounding will ensure reliability in subjective scoring by adhering to standardized evaluation parameters.' This implies a structured protocol including inter-rater reliability measures; the omission of specifics is a description gap, not an absence of protocol. In practice, academic NLP evaluation routinely uses crowd-sourced raters with Cohen's kappa or Krippendorff's alpha, and these methods can be applied here. On the ROUGE/human misalignment: ROUGE is used only for model selection on the validation set, not as a reporting metric. Using a computationally cheap, automated proxy for model selection is pragmatic, and the final reported comparison will use human scores. The risk that ROUGE-optimal differs from human-optimal models is real but manageable if human evaluation is also conducted on validation candidates. On abstract quality as labels: this concern applies equally to all abstractive summarization research and does not differentially disadvantage this study; it reflects the state of the field and affects both the proposed model and the baseline. On author clustering: this is a valid concern for datasets with prolific authors and structured writing styles. On mode preference confounding: this is the most fundamental concern—human preference for abstractive style means the baseline comparison may not isolate the contribution of the hierarchical mechanism specifically.

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues remain inadequately resolved. First, the ROUGE/human misalignment: the defender suggests conducting human evaluation on validation candidates too, but this is not in the design—it would require substantial additional annotation effort not budgeted or described. Without this, the model selection criterion and the reporting metric are formally decoupled, which is a protocol flaw. Second, author/paper clustering: the defense did not address this. Papers from the same research group share vocabulary, citation patterns, and structural conventions; without cluster-aware splitting, the model may partially memorize these idiosyncrasies and appear more generalizable than it is. Third, mode preference confounding: the defense acknowledged this is a concern but did not propose a resolution. Without ablating the abstractive vs. hierarchical component separately (e.g., comparing a flat attention abstractive model vs. hierarchical attention abstractive model, both against the extractive baseline), the hypothesis about the benefit of hierarchical attention specifically cannot be isolated.
round_transcripts.defender: The defender concedes that the mode preference confounding is a genuine threat to the hypothesis. A flat abstractive model as an additional baseline would be needed to isolate the contribution of the hierarchical mechanism. The author clustering concern also stands—a deduplication step at the research group level should be applied before splitting. The ROUGE/human misalignment is a practical tradeoff that weakens but does not invalidate the experiment; however, the design as written does not formally guarantee human-optimal model selection. These three issues require design refinements rather than empirical resolution, as they affect what question the experiment actually answers.

all_issues_adjudicated: ROUGE-based model selection misaligns with human evaluation as the primary metric
No author/paper-level deduplication in the split—same-author papers in train and test inflate scores
Extractive vs. abstractive baseline conflates architectural mode preference with summarization quality
